{{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

{{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

{{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}
[[Category:History of Japanese astronomy]] [[Category:Chinese astronomy]] [[Category:Ritsuryō system]] [[Category:Heian and Nara court]] [[Category:Astrology]] [[ja:天文密奏]]

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Mitsusō'''|てんもんみつそう}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{Ruby|'''Tenmon Sō'''|てんもんそう}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family traditions|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. *
