[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also! == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}
[[Category:History of Japanese astronomy]] [[Category:Chinese astronomy]] [[Category:Ritsuryō system]] [[Category:Heian and Nara court]] [[Category:Astrology]] [[ja:天文密奏]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}
[[Category:History of Japanese astronomy]] [[Category:Chinese astronomy]] [[Category:Ritsuryō system]] [[Category:Heian and Nara court]] [[Category:Astrology]] [[ja:天文密奏]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|天文博士" to "|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase"
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}
[[Category:History of Japanese astronomy]] [[Category:Chinese astronomy]] [[Category:Ritsuryō system]] [[Category:Heian and Nara court]] [[Category:Astrology]] [[ja:天文密奏]]
* [[Abe no Seimei]] * [[Da Liu Ren]] * [[Goryō]] * [[Itako]] * [[Kuji-in]] * [[Onmyōdō]] * [[Sanpaku]] * [[Seimei Shrine]] * [[Senji Ryakketsu]] * [[Shikigami]] * [[Shinigami]] * [[Tengenjutsu (fortune telling)]] * [[Ushi no toki mairi]]

View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tenmon Missō]][[en:draft:Tenmon Missō]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tenmon Missō|Tenmon Missō]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} in ancient {{ill|East Asia|ja|東アジア}}. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.
== Overview == In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the {{ill|state|ja|国家}} on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the {{ill|Son of Heaven|ja|天子}} to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The {{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in {{ill|astronomy|ja|天文学}} and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."
In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}}, the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記}}.
The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経}}, {{ill|Senji Ryakketsu|ja|占事略决}}, and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林}}.
After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}}, who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄}} from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}, or regent, without concern for their attire.
Over time, during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights. * == See Also == * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}

'''天文密奏'''（てんもんみっそう）とは、[[古代]]の[[東アジア]]において、異常な[[天文現象]]が観測された場合に、その観測記録と[[占星術]]による解釈（占言）を内密に[[君主]]に対して[[上奏]]することを指す。'''天文奏'''（てんもんそう）とも。
== 概要 == 古代[[中国]]においては、天上の星は地上にある[[国家]]を象徴しており、その動きに異常があれば、政治的な異変が発生する前兆現象であるととらえられていた（[[上天]]思想）。そのため、それを一刻も早く君主が把握する事は、国政の運営上重要な事であると考えられていた。また、君主が天文現象に現れる天の意思を把握してその運行に基づいた正しい暦・時法を定めて民に授けることは「[[天子]]」としての責務であると考えられていた。[[律令制]]下の[[日本]]でも[[天文道]]・[[暦道]]の教科書として用いられていた『[[晋書]]』天文志には「天文異変には吉兆もあるが、多くが災異の前兆である」と記されている。このため、異常な天文現象に対しては、[[天文学]]と占星術に通じた者が君主に対してその事実と[[占い]]の結果を報告することが求められた。これが天文密奏である。
日本では、[[天文博士]]（権博士を含む）あるいは天文密奏[[宣旨]]を受けた天文学・占星術に通じた人物が、異常な天文現象を見つけた際には、占いの結果と過去に発生した同様の事例から勘案してその意味を解釈したものを観測記録とともに奏書に認めて密封して、速やかに[[陰陽寮|陰陽頭]]を通じて[[天皇]]に対して上奏されることとなっていた。[[養老律令]]の[[雑令]]には「秘書玄象条」があり、「若し微祥災異有らば、[[陰陽寮]]奏せよ。訖らば季別に封して[[中務省]]に送り、国史に入れよ」とある。天文に関する項目は「天子」である天皇自身に帰属する責務であったため、国政の最高機関である[[太政官]]を経由しない上奏が許されていたのである。ただし、後世には太政官の[[一上]]（あるいは[[摂政・関白の一覧|摂関]]）が事前に内容を確認して改めて密封を行って天文博士に返却してから改めて陰陽頭に提出する形式となる（『[[西宮記]]』・『[[新儀式]]』・『[[禁秘抄]]』など）。また、国史（担当は[[内記]]）は占文部分は重大な機密として記録しないものとされていた。
なお、この時占いに用いられた書として『[[天文要録]]』・『[[新撰陰陽書]]』・『[[帝覧]]』・『[[天文録]]』・『[[天地瑞祥志]]』・『[[指掌宿曜経]]』・『[[占事略决]]』・『[[暦林]]』などが知られている。
ただし、[[蔵人|蔵人所]]成立後は、天文博士らは陰陽頭を通さずに[[蔵人]]に対して密奏を提出して、蔵人から天皇に報告するようになった。ただし事の性格上、深夜あるいは早朝に密奏が行われる場合もあり、その場合は天文博士が直接天皇に上奏する事も例外的に認められていた。鎌倉時代の『禁秘抄』には、正副天文博士および密奏資格者は天変発生時には直ちに密奏を[[内覧]]すなわち摂関に進めること、その際には密奏者の服装・格好は問われないことが記されている。
また、上奏する資格も[[平安時代]]中期以後には天文博士を世襲した[[阿倍氏|安倍氏]]と[[外記|局務]]の[[中原氏]]の両家のみに限定されるようになっていく（なお、中原氏の天文密奏は[[醍醐天皇]]の時代の[[中原以忠|十市部以忠（十市以忠）]]（後に[[天禄]]2年（[[972年]]）に宗家の[[中原有象|十市有象]]とともに中原の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられて同氏の祖となる）に遡り、同氏本来の[[外記]]の職務とは別に同氏が[[家学]]としていた[[明経道]]の出典である漢籍には天文現象の解釈に関する記述が含まれていることが多く、天文道に通じた人物を輩出することが多かった事によるとされている）。なお、[[室町時代]]には[[暦博士]]の[[賀茂朝臣氏|賀茂氏]]に対しても天文密奏宣旨を下された例がある。『[[吾妻鏡]]』によると、[[鎌倉幕府]]でも天文道に通じた[[陰陽師]]を[[京都]]から[[鎌倉]]へ招いて[[征夷大将軍|将軍]]に対して天文密奏を行わせたとされている（こうした場合、朝廷に対する密奏が不可能となるため、密奏宣旨は天文博士などへ昇進するための資格を得る以上の意味はなかったと考えられている）。
なお、全ての異常な天文現象が天文密奏の対象になったわけではなく、天文博士・天文密奏宣旨授与者が占った結果、重大な変事の前兆と判断された出来事に限定され、天文博士・天文密奏宣旨授与者が異常の発生に気付かなかった場合も密奏は行われなかった。『[[後二条師通記]]』[[永長]]元年[[8月13日_(旧暦)|8月13日_]]条（[[嘉保]]3年/[[1096年]][[9月2日]]）によれば、8月に入って夜空に[[流星]]が見えて人々が大騒ぎをしているのに、天文密奏が無かった。そこで、[[関白]][[藤原師通]]らが、天文密奏宣旨授与者である[[中原師遠]]と天文博士[[安倍親宗]]を喚問したところ、師遠は流星に気付かずに観測が出来なかったために密奏が出来ず、親宗は天文異変ではないので天文密奏の必要性は無かったと回答している。
もっとも、当時の天文道・暦道は[[日食]]の発生の予知の正確さについてはかなり高かったものの、その他の分野に関しては毎日[[戌|戌の刻]]と[[寅|寅の刻]]に行われる定時観測を維持するだけの人員と設備しかなかったと考えられており、天文博士らが確実に異常な天文現象を観測できる保証が無かったために、実際には異常な天文現象が発生しても、天文密奏が行われなかった例も相当数あったと考えられている。
更に平安時代末期になると、安倍氏によって天文博士の地位と天文密奏宣旨の独占が行われるようになるが、安倍氏のもう1つの家学である[[陰陽道]]との密着と安倍氏内部の内紛が天文密奏に混乱を与えた。[[元暦]]2年（[[1185年]]）元旦に出現した赤気を巡って密奏を行った[[安倍季弘]]兄弟と他の安倍氏一族との間で論争が発生した。前者は彗星と論じ、後者は[[蚩尤旗]]であると唱えた。[[右大臣]][[九条兼実]]の諮問を受けた季弘は、[[治承]]元年（[[1177年]]）にも同じようなことがあり、亡くなった父[[安倍泰親]]が彗星といい、自分が蚩尤旗であるとしたので、父が天に判断を仰ぎ誤った方に天罰が下ることを祈請したところ、自分が重病になったので彗星だと判明した。従って実際の星の動きは問題ではないと唱えたという（『[[玉葉]]』正暦2年[[1月12日 (旧暦)|正月（1月）12日]]条）。これは天文道及び天文密奏が次第に実際の星の動きを見て占うという中国由来の上天思想的な解釈から日本独自に発達していった占いの技法である陰陽道的な解釈へと変化していく状況を示した事例であると考えられている。また、[[官司請負制]]の下で安倍氏は天文道の知識をもって天文密奏を請負う立場にあったが、一族間の共同作業を要する賀茂氏（暦道）の造暦と異なり、天文密奏は単独で行うことも可能であった。そのため、安倍氏に属する親子間で知識・学説を継承しつづけることによって個々の家に独特の学説が生じるようになった（安倍氏の天文道の場合、安倍泰親を祖とする嫡流（泰親流）・[[安倍晴道]]を祖とする晴道党・[[安倍広賢]]（[[安倍宗明|宗明]]の子）を祖とする宗明流の3派が代表的な存在であった）。朝廷では3派の対立の緩和の一方で、その技術力の維持・向上と3派間の相互判定を目的としてそれぞれの派の有力者に天文密奏宣旨を与えた。更に[[天福 (日本)|天福]]2年（[[1235年]]）には宣旨を受けていない[[安倍家氏]]（宗明流）が客星出現を知らせた功績で天文博士に補されるという本来の天文博士が天文密奏を行うのとは逆の事例も生じている（『[[明月記]]』[[文暦]]2年[[3月26日 (旧暦)|3月26日]]条）。
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=細井浩志|authorlink=細井浩志|title=古代の天文異変と史書|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2007|isbn=978-4-642-02462-4|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=山下克明|authorlink=山下克明|title=平安時代の宗教文化と陰陽道|publisher=岩田書院|year=1996|isbn=978-4-900697-65-2|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=赤澤春彦|authorlink=赤澤春彦|title=鎌倉期官人陰陽師の研究|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2011|isbn=978-4-642-02893-6|ref=harv}}
== 関連項目 == *[[陰陽寮]]

{{nihongo|'''Tenmon Missō'''|天文密奏}} refers to the secret reporting to a ruler of observations of abnormal {{ill|astronomical phenomena|ja|天文現象|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/astronomical phenomena|qq=|1=astronomical phenomenon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q751989}} and their interpretations (predictions) based on [[:en:Astrology|astrology]] in ancient [[:en:East Asia|East Asia]]. It is also known as {{nihongo|'''Tenmon Sō'''|天文奏}}.

== Overview ==
In ancient {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, the stars in the heavens were thought to symbolize the [[:en:State (polity)|state]] on Earth, and any anomalies in their movements were perceived as portents of political upheaval ({{ill|Mandate of Heaven|ja|上天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandate of Heaven|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} thought). Therefore, it was considered crucial for the ruler to promptly grasp such celestial events as a matter of state governance. Furthermore, it was deemed the duty of the [[:en:Son of Heaven|Son of Heaven]] to interpret the heavenly will manifest in astronomical phenomena, establish correct calendars and timekeeping, and bestow them upon the people. The [[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]’s "Astronomical Records," used as a textbook for {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], states that "astronomical anomalies may be omens of fortune, but most are portents of disaster." Thus, it became necessary for those versed in [[:en:Astronomy|astronomy]] and astrology to report such anomalies and their interpretations to the ruler. This practice was termed "Tenmon Mitsusō."

In Japan, when abnormalities were observed, {{ill|Tenmon Hakase|ja|天文博士|1=Tenmon Hakase|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmon Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmon Hakase|qq=}} (including assistant Tenmon Hakase) or individuals granted a Tenmon Mitsusō {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} were required to interpret the phenomena based on past cases and submit a sealed report, through the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}, to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. The [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]’s {{ill|Zatsuryō|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zatsuryō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specifies: "If there are minor auspicious or inauspicious events, report them to the Onmyōryō. Afterward, seal them seasonally and send them to the Ministry of the Interior to be archived in the national records." This bypassing of the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]], the highest organ of governance, reflected the emperor's exclusive responsibility for astronomical matters. In later periods, the content of these reports was reviewed and resealed by the Daijōkan or {{ill|Sesshō and Kampaku|ja|摂政・関白|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshō and Kampaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} before being resubmitted to the Onmyōryō (as described in works such as {{ill|Saigūki|ja|西宮記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūki|qq=|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}}, {{ill|Shingi-shiki|ja|新儀式|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi-shiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinjishō|qq=|1=Kinpishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591153}}). Portions of the reports pertaining to predictions were deemed state secrets and excluded from official records managed by {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naiki|qq=}}.

The documents used for such divinations included the {{ill|Tenmon Yōroku|ja|天文要録|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmon Yōroku|qq=|1=Tenmon Yōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11442665}}, {{ill|Shinsen Onmyō-sho|ja|新撰陰陽書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Onmyō-sho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Teiran|ja|帝覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teiran|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tenmonroku|ja|天文録|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmonroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tenchi Zuisenshō|ja|天地瑞祥志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenchi Zuisenshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|ja|指掌宿曜経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishō Sukuyō-kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Senji Ryakketsu|Senji Ryakketsu]], and {{ill|Rekirin|ja|暦林|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekirin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

After the establishment of the {{ill|Kurōdodokoro|ja|蔵人所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurōdodokoro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵人|1=Kurōdo-dokoro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6447696}}, Tenmon Hakase bypassed the Onmyōryō and submitted reports directly to the [[:en:Kurōdo-dokoro|Kurōdo]], who relayed them to the emperor. In urgent cases, such as late at night or early morning, Tenmon Hakase were allowed to report directly to the emperor. The {{ill|Kinjishō|ja|禁秘抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinjishō|qq=|1=Kinpishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591153}} from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] notes that Tenmon Hakase and qualified secret envoys must immediately report any celestial phenomena to the [[:en:Nairan|Nairan]], or regent, without concern for their attire.

Over time, during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Tenmon Mitsusō privileges became restricted to the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}, both of which had deep connections to {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family knowledge|qq=}} of astrology and astronomy. Notably, during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], even the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, associated with calendar-making, were occasionally granted Tenmon Mitsusō rights.
*
